text: The surgeon performs a preperitoneal dissection, carefully peeling away the areolar tissue from the abdominal wall, revealing a shiny fascial layer above.

The surgeon strips the robust areolar tissue, resembling white cotton candy, from the fascia, revealing the thin peritoneum beneath. As the dissection progresses laterally, the preperitoneal plane becomes easier to develop, with increased visibility of fat tissue.

The surgeon uses the left hand for retraction to reveal the dissection plane around the hernia sac. The recurrent inguinal hernias are small but visible. The surgeon aims for a five to six centimeter mesh overlap, creating a large pocket for potential adjustments.

In the groin area, the surgeon carefully dissects along the peritoneum, separating it from the abdominal wall while leaving some fat attached to the wall. The hernia sac is visible and is being reduced, showing a clear sac structure that facilitates the reduction process.

The surgeon uses scissors as a hot scalpel to score tissue layers, providing hemostatic dissection with the left hand maintaining good retraction. The dissection progresses past the hernia, revealing normal tissue planes, and begins to approach the peritoneal side.

The surgeon continues dissection to achieve more mesh overlap, revealing a small hernia with fatty tissue protruding through the abdominal wall. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible on the right, and the dissection progresses towards the testicular vessels.

The surgeon performs a buccal space dissection in the groin area, carefully staying against the peritoneum. Extra peritoneal fat is left against the abdominal wall to avoid entering the plane where named vessels, such as the femoral branch and lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, are located.

The surgeon dissects medially, skeletonizing the vas deferens as part of the inguinal hernia repair. The core structures are visible, and the procedure follows step five of the critical view of the malpector and the orifice.
The surgeon uses good retraction and hemostasis to quickly proceed to the area with abundant fat on the superior pubic rami and pubic tubercle, cutting through the fat to expose Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon follows an areolar trail to guide the dissection, completing step five of the procedure. The focus is on ensuring good mesh overlap while examining the cord for any lipoma, which is absent.

The surgeon reviews the anatomy, highlighting the interstitial hernia located above the arcuate line. Testicular vessels are visible within the vas wall, and the internal ring has been skeletonized. The infra-epigastric vessels are dissected in the retropubic space, extending nearly two centimeters deep towards the obturator space. The surgeon prepares to close the defect.
The surgeon performs additional dissection before closing the defect using an Ovilac 180 suture. The suture is used to grasp superficial tissue, likely the external oblique, avoiding deep bites.

The surgeon uses a suture to obliterate the dead space, ensuring efficient handling of the needle by maintaining grip and following the arc of the needle with the left hand. The loop is freed after getting caught, and the needle is parked when using both hands to tighten the suture.
The surgeon runs the suture back a few throws to lock it in place, ensuring secure closure of the defect.

The surgeon measures the inguinal space to determine if a 16x12 Progrip mesh will fit over the interstitial hernia. The mesh provides adequate overlap, and the surgeon sutures it in place using 3-0 Vial to secure the mesh and prevent movement as the patient recovers.

The surgeon sets the lower flap of the ProGrip mesh first, then unfolds the upper flap to cover the interstitial hernia, indirect space, direct space, and femoral space. The mesh is secured with three stitches to prevent shifting, especially laterally where it may not adhere well to the shiny transverse salus fascia of the transverse abdominis.

The surgeon completes the closure of the flap using a six-inch 3-0 V-Lock 180 suture, efficiently maneuvering the needle with the left hand to assist in the flap closure. The mesh appears well-positioned and secure, eliminating the need for pocket evacuation.
